---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
library(kableExtra)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(countrycode)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/00-Leviathan_Cover_55.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size35[**II. How and why do governments use violence against the people inside their borders?**]]

<br>

.size45[

**Today's Agenda**: Measuring "Political Violence"

- The CIRIGHTS Data project
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Fall 2023)
]]

???

### Prep for Class
1. ...

<br>

**SLIDE**: Today we continue our work on the first paper...




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.content-box-white[**Paper 1**]]

.size35[
If someone came to you with the goal of better understanding the use of political violence by governments around the world, which of the data sources that we explored in class would you recommend and why? 

Your report should introduce each source to the reader with your analysis of its strengths and weaknesses. 

Ultimately, your central argument should be a clear recommendation of which source(s) they should focus on.
]

???

### Questions on the prompt?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size50[.content-box-purple[**Sources of Political Violence Data**]]

.size35[
- The US State Department's "Country Reports on Human Rights Practices"

- Amnesty International's "Annual Country Reports"

- **The Political Terror Scale (PTS)**

- The CIRIGHTS data project's "Physical Integrity Rights"

- Varieties of Democracy's (V-Dem) "Personal Integrity Rights"
]

???

Last class we explored the Political Terror Scale (PTS)

### Key takeaways? 

### - e.g. elements of validity and reliability arguments that stood out for you?

### - How do the PTS scores supplement your reading/analysis of the source docs?

<br>

+ PTS represents expert codings by area experts (right?) with reliability checks built-in. Using these measures backs up YOUR analysis of the source docs. It's not just you interpreting them this way but backed by experts!

+ PTS enables and facilitates comparisons, but not just across countries. Your reports analyze a single country across time which is a comparison exercise. You are comparing your country to itself each year through time!

+ BUT as we noted PTS is limited. 
    + Five point scale but is it safe to assume that holds for all people in the country? White dude watching TV vs LGBTQ+ person in a protest against the government?
    + PTS focuses pretty narrowly on government violence of a particular type. Leaves out much we might argue is important to measuring political violence.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size60[.center[.content-box-white[**For Today**]]]

.size50[
**Explore the data and codebook for the [CIRIGHTS Data project](https://www.dropbox.com/sh/t8utmzsvde8m63q/AAAs1_WIJTqXurAE5nvEKWE5a?dl=0).**

Make sure you understand the observations and variables enough to play with them in class.
]

???

### Everybody ready for today's work?

<br>

Before we dive into the data we have to evaluate its validity and reliability.

### So, let's begin in this same place we've begun with the other sources, what is the concept the CIRIGHTS project is trying to measure?

- (**SLIDE**)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle


```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='75%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/05-2-CIRIGHTS_Logo.png')
```

<br>

.size50[
.center[.content-box-white[**"...government respect for human rights (p2)"**]]
]

???

### And why is this their focus? What is their primary data source?
- (The US State Department Country Reports on Human Rights Practices!)
    - Although, note on p1 that they also use AI reports for the "Personal Integrity Rights" scores

<br>

Everybody open up the ToC in the CIRIGHTS codebook.

<br>

As you can see in the codebook's ToC the CIRIGHTS project divides "respect for human rights" into four broad categories

- Moving forward we'll be focusing on the "Physical integrity rights" index which I think makes the most sense given our work on Monday's cases.

- However, in thinking about your group definitions of political violence...

### Are there other measures listed here that might be useful measures of political violence? Why or why not?

### - Maybe an element from the Empowerment Rights Index?

- Foreign Movement, 
- Domestic Movement, 
- Freedom of Speech, 
- Freedom of Assembly & Association, Workers’ Rights, Electoral Self-Determination, and Freedom of Religion indicators

<br>

**SLIDE**: Ok, let's unpack the "Physical integrity rights"


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size35[.content-box-white[**Measuring "Physical Integrity Rights" (CIRIGHTS)**]]]

.pull-left[
.center[.size40[
**Operationalization**

<html>&#8595;</html>

**Instrumentation**

<html>&#8595;</html>

**Measurement**
]]]

.pull-right[

.size35[
<br>
]

.size40[
1. Disappearance

2. Extrajudicial Killing

3. Political Imprisonment

4. Torture
]
]

???

*Split class into 4 groups: One group per measure*

GROUPS: Make an argument about the validity and reliability of your assigned measure based on the operationalization, instrumentation and measurement described in the codebook.

- Build strengths and weaknesses lists directly on the board

<br>

### Questions on the assignment?

- Get to it!

<br>

**SLIDES** for each



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size40[.content-box-white[**Measuring "Physical Integrity Rights" (CIRIGHTS)**]]]

<br>

.size40[
"**Disappearances** are cases in which people have disappeared, agents of the state are likely responsible, and political motivation may be likely" (p8).

- (0) Have occurred frequently

- (1) Have occurred occasionally

- (2) Have not occurred / Unreported
]

???

Validity and Reliability

Strengths
- ?

Weaknesses
- ?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size40[.content-box-white[**Measuring "Physical Integrity Rights" (CIRIGHTS)**]]]

<br>

.size40[
"**Extrajudicial killings** are killings by government officials without due process of law" (p6).

- (0) Practiced frequently

- (1) Practiced occasionally

- (2) Have not occurred / Unreported
]

???

Validity and Reliability

Strengths
- ?

Weaknesses
- ?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size40[.content-box-white[**Measuring "Physical Integrity Rights" (CIRIGHTS)**]]]

<br>

.size35[
"**Political imprisonment** refers to the incarceration of people by government officials because of their speech; their non-violent opposition to government policies or leaders; their religious beliefs; their non-violent religious practices including proselytizing; or their membership in a group, including an ethnic or racial group" (p13).]

.size35[
- (0) Yes, and many

- (1) Yes, but few

- (2) None / None Reported
]

???

Validity and Reliability

Strengths
- ?

Weaknesses
- ?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size40[.content-box-white[**Measuring "Physical Integrity Rights" (CIRIGHTS)**]]]

<br>

.size35[
"**Torture** refers to the purposeful inflicting of extreme pain, whether mental or physical, by government officials or by private individuals at the instigation of government officials" (p11).]

.size35[
- (0) Practiced frequently

- (1) Practiced occasionally

- (2) Not practiced / Unreported
]

???

Validity and Reliability

Strengths
- ?

Weaknesses
- ?





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size45[.content-box-white[**Measuring the "Physical Integrity Rights Index"**]]]

.pull-left[

.size40[
<br>
]

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/05-2-CIRIGHTS_Logo.png')
```
]

.pull-right[

.size25[
<br>
]

.size45[
- Disappearance

- Extrajudicial Killing

- Political Imprisonment

- Torture
]]

???

Let's now audit the data.

- Everybody focus on the two countries you've been analyzing this week and last.

### Would you have coded these the same way? Why or why not?

<br>

### Findings? Takeaways?

#### - Which variables seem the easiest to code / we have the most confidence in?

#### - Which the least?

<br>

### Open it up to the class: What did you find or notice when exploring the dataset?

### - Show us something cool!

- *Let them lead this and after a while feel free to segue to your slides*

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's continue to audit the data



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size70[
.center[Any missing data issues in the 2020 "Physical Integrity Rights Index"?]

- e.g. selection bias
]

???

Let's dig into the data.

- REMEMBER, our key exercise here is to analyze the validity and reliability of the measures SO THAT later we can use them to analyze country behavior.

<br>

Use the autofilter to zoom in on 2020 only

- Then scroll through and talk to me about the kinds of countries with missing data in any of the three measures.



---

class: slideblue, middle

```{r}
d <- read_excel("../../Data/CIRIGHTS/cirights-v1.25.22.11/CIRIGHTS V1.25.22.11/cirights_v1.22.11.25.xlsx")

d2020 <- d |>
  filter(year == 2020)

#View(d2020 |> select(country, disap, kill, polpris, tort))

# Descriptive Stats Table
x1 <- d2020 |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = polpris:kill, names_to = "Source", values_to = "Value") |>
  group_by(Source) |>
  summarize(
    N = n(),
    NAs = sum(is.na(Value)),
    Prop = str_c(round(NAs/N, 2)*100, '%'),
    #Mean = round(mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    #StdDev = round(sd(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    #Min = round(min(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 0),
    #Max = round(max(Value, na.rm = TRUE), 0)
  ) 

x1 |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c('l', rep('c', 3)), col.names = c("Source", "N", "Missing", "Missing (%)")) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), font_size = 22) |>
  column_spec(column = 1:4, width = "12em")
```

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='80%', fig.height=4.5, fig.width=9.5, eval=TRUE, cache=FALSE}
## Make a map
## Use rnaturalearth to define world map data
worldmap <- ne_countries(scale = 'medium', type = 'countries', returnclass = 'sf')

# # Identify merging country label
# guess_field(d2020$country)
# guess_field(d2020$iso_a3) # 100% genc3c, iso3c, wb
# guess_field(d2020$ciri)
# guess_field(d2020$cow) # 99.5% cown
# guess_field(d2020$polity)
# guess_field(d2020$unctry) # 98% iso3n
# guess_field(d2020$unreg)
# guess_field(worldmap$adm0_a3) #95.8% iso3c
# 
# # Add newcodes
# d2020$newcode1 <- countrycode(d2020$iso_a3, origin = "iso3c", destination = "iso3c")
# 
# worldmap$newcode1 <- countrycode(worldmap$adm0_a3, origin = "iso3c", destination = "iso3c")
#d10 <- left_join(worldmap, d2020, by = "newcode1")

# Merge
d2020_1 <- d2020 |>
  select(country, iso_a3, physint_sum, polpris:kill) |>
  mutate(
    Missing = 0
  )

d10 <- left_join(worldmap, d2020_1, by = c("adm0_a3" = "iso_a3"))

# Audit missing data
# d2020_1 |>
#   select(country, physint_sum, polpris:kill, Missing) |>
#   View()
# 
# tibble(d10) |>
#   select(name, physint_sum, polpris:kill, Missing) |>
#   filter(is.na(Missing)) |>
#   print(n = 100)
# 
# tibble(d10)
# tibble(d10) |> count(Missing)


d10$Missing[d10$name == "Kosovo"] <- 0
d10$Missing[d10$name == "S. Sudan"] <- 0
d10$Missing[d10$name == "Serbia"] <- 0


d10 |>
  mutate(
    Missing = if_else(is.na(Missing), "Missing Data", "Data Collected")
  ) |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = factor(Missing))) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "CIRIGHTS Missing Data (2020)") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 2, direction = -1)
```

???

### Why is there no missing data here?

- (Source is the State Dept Reports which now cover all the UN member states+)





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
How much do the measures overlap in 2020? 

Do we need all four sub-measures or is the index sufficient? 
]

???

### Scroll through the 2020 scores, do states that score badly on one measure score badly on all of them?

### Any pairings of measures that move together? e.g. states that disappear people also torture?

*Give them time to review the data and answer*




---

class: middle, slideblue

.center[.size55[.content-box-white[**Correlations Across the Measures**]]]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center'}
d2020 |>
  select(disap, kill, polpris, tort) |>
  cor() |>
  kbl(digits = 2, align = c(rep('c', 5))) |>
  column_spec(column = 1:5, width = "5em") |>
  kable_styling(font_size = 40)
```

???

Let's use some more of our Data Analysis tools! 

<br>

### Remind me, what does correlation measure?
- (a measure of linear association)

<br>

### And how do we interpret a correlation coefficient?

<br>

### So, are these four measures associated with each other?

### Which pairings "most" and which "least" correlated?

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's use the data to describe the world.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
Which of the four types of "Physical Integrity Rights" were most commonly violated in 2020?
]

???

*Give them time to review the data and answer*



---

class: full, slideblue
class: middle

```{r, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.6, out.width='100%', fig.width=9, cache=TRUE}
# Table data (counts and proportions)
tab1 <- d2020 |>
  select(disap, kill, polpris, tort) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = disap:tort, names_to = "Measure", values_to = "Values") |>
  group_by(Measure) |>
  count(Values)

tab2 <- tab1 |>
  mutate(
    total = sum(n),
    prop = n/total
  ) |>
  select(Measure, Values, prop) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Values, values_from = prop, names_prefix = "Score ") 

# Bar plot
d2020 |>
  select(disap, kill, polpris, tort) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = disap:tort, names_to = "Measure", values_to = "Values") |>
  group_by(Measure) |>
  count(Values) |>
  mutate(
    Values = case_when(
      Values == 0 ~ "Score 0",
      Values == 1 ~ "Score 1",
      Values == 2 ~ "Score 2"
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Measure, y = n, fill = Values)) +
  #geom_col(color = "grey", width = .6, position = position_fill(reverse = TRUE)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", color = "grey", width = .6) +
  #geom_hline(yintercept = c(25, 50, 75, 100), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  #scale_fill_manual(values = c("red", "orange", "green3"))
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 5) +
  labs(x = "", y = "", fill = "",
       title = "Physical Integrity Rights by Country",
       caption = "Source: CIRIGHTS 2020") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c('disap', 'polpris', 'kill', 'tort'),
                   labels = c("Disappearances", "Political Prisoners", "Killings", "Torture")) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

???

Here's a simple bar plot of state scores on the four measures in 2020.

### Interpret this simple bar plot for me. What does this show us about states' preferred tools of violence in 2020?

<br>

**SLIDE**: One other way to visualize this data



---

class: full, slideblue
class: middle

```{r, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.6, out.width='100%', fig.width=9, cache=TRUE}
# Bar plot
d2020 |>
  select(disap, kill, polpris, tort) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = disap:tort, names_to = "Measure", values_to = "Values") |>
  group_by(Measure) |>
  count(Values) |>
  mutate(
    Values = case_when(
      Values == 0 ~ "Score 0",
      Values == 1 ~ "Score 1",
      Values == 2 ~ "Score 2"
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Measure, y = n, fill = Values)) +
  geom_col(color = "grey", width = .6, position = position_fill(reverse = TRUE)) +
  #geom_col(position = "dodge", color = "grey", width = .6) +
  #geom_hline(yintercept = c(25, 50, 75, 100), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  #scale_fill_manual(values = c("red", "orange", "green3"))
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 5) +
  labs(x = "", y = "", fill = "",
       title = "Physical Integrity Rights by Country",
       caption = "Source: CIRIGHTS 2020") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c('disap', 'polpris', 'kill', 'tort'),
                   labels = c("Disappearances", "Political Prisoners", "Killings", "Torture")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  annotate("text", x = 1:4, y = .05, label = str_c(round(tab2$`Score 0`, 2)*100, '%'))
```

???

This stacked bar plot represents country scores as a proportion out of 100%

- Here we see the breakdown of scores per violence tool

<br>

This is a staggering result for torture!
- 81/195 torture frequently (41% of countries in 2020)!

- 81% use torture 'frequently' or 'occasionally'!

<br>

### Any hypotheses as to why torture is the most common form of government violence?

<br>

### Any idea why disappearances appear to be the most rare form of government violence?


<br>

```{r}
tab1 |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Values, values_from = n, names_prefix = "Score ") |>
  kbl(align = 'c') |>
  column_spec(column = 1:4, width = "3em")

tab2 |>
  kbl(align = 'c', digits = 2) |>
  column_spec(column = 1:4, width = "3em")
```






---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
Any countries with a total index score that challenges your intuitions? 
]

???

*Give them time to review the data and answer*



---

class: full, middle, slideblue

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.height=5, fig.width=9.5, eval=TRUE, cache=TRUE}
## Make a map
# Merge
d10 <- left_join(worldmap, d2020, by = c("adm0_a3" = "iso_a3"))

# Audit missing data
# d2020 |>
#   select(country, physint_sum, polpris:kill) |>
#   View()
# 
# tibble(d10) |>
#   select(name, physint_sum, polpris:kill) |>
#   filter(is.na(physint_sum)) |>
#   print(n = 100)

d10$physint_sum[d10$name == "Kosovo"] <- 7
d10$physint_sum[d10$name == "S. Sudan"] <- 0
d10$physint_sum[d10$name == "Serbia"] <- 6

d10 |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = factor(physint_sum))) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "physint_sum (2020)") +
  #theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 5, na.value="darkgrey")
```

???

### Any surprises here?

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's compare PTS and CIRIGHTS




---

class: middle

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='61%', fig.asp=.45, fig.width = 9, cache=FALSE}
pts <- read_excel("../../Data/PTS/2023-05-PTS_2022/PTS-2022.xlsx", na = "NA") |>
  filter(Year == 2020)

## Use rnaturalearth to define world map data
worldmap <- ne_countries(scale = 'medium', type = 'countries', returnclass = 'sf')

pts$newcode1 <- countrycode::countrycode(pts$COW_Code_A, origin = "cowc", destination = "iso3c")
worldmap$newcode1 <- countrycode::countrycode(worldmap$adm0_a3, origin = "iso3c", destination = "iso3c")

# Merge data onto worldmap
d100 <- left_join(worldmap, pts, by = "newcode1")

d100 |>
  mutate(
    pts_s_cat = factor(PTS_S, levels = c("1", "2", "3", "4", "5"))
  ) |>
  #filter(!is.na(pts_s_cat)) |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = pts_s_cat)) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "PTS_S (2020)") +
  #theme(legend.position = "right") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("royalblue", "skyblue", "lemonchiffon1", "sandybrown", "red3"))
```

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='61%', fig.asp=.45, fig.width = 9, cache=FALSE}
d10 |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = factor(physint_sum))) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "physint_sum (2020)") +
  #theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 5, na.value="darkgrey")
```

???

Annoying that CIRIGHTS scores work on the inverse to PTS.

- I prefer measures to increase with "more" of the name of the variable
    - e.g. higher torture score should mean more torture
    
- But they didn't ask us so this is what we have (unless we recode it!)

<br>

### What do we learn from comparing and contrasting these global scores?

<br>

### - Any surprises?

### - Lessons for us re reliability / validity?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
How has the US experience of political violence changed across time?
]

???

Everybody zoom in on the US observations across time and get ready to describe our experience of political violence.

<br>

*Give them time to review the data and answer*




---

class: middle, slideblue

.pull-left[
.center[.size40[.content-box-white[**USA in CIRIGHTS**]]]

<br>

```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='100%', fig.width=5.5, fig.align='center', fig.asp=.8, cache=TRUE}
d |>
  filter(iso_a3 == "USA") |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = physint_sum)) +
  geom_line(size = .4) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Physical Integrity Rights") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:8, labels = 0:8, limits = c(0,8))
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='80%', fig.width=7, fig.align='center', fig.asp=1.5, cache=TRUE}
## You make a line plot of averages across time for each measure
d |>
  filter(iso_a3 == "USA") |>
  pivot_longer(cols = polpris:kill, names_to = "Variables", values_to = "Values") |>
  mutate(
    Variables = case_when(
      Variables == "disap" ~ "Disappearances",
      Variables == "kill" ~ "Killings",
      Variables == "tort" ~ "Torture",
      Variables == "polpris" ~ "Political Prisoners"
    )
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = Values, color = Variables)) +
  geom_line(size = .4) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  facet_wrap(~ Variables, ncol = 1) +
  guides(color = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:2, labels = 0:2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1980, 2020, 4)) +
  labs(x = "") +
  scale_color_brewer(type = "qual", palette = 2)
```
]

???

### What do we think of the scores for the US over time?

<br>

### Any idea where this data comes from if not from the State Department?
- Must be AI?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[**For Next Class**]

.size60[
Be ready to evaluate and explore the V-Dem Varieties of Democracy "Personal Integrity Rights" data
]

???

For Friday we repeat today's work on the CIRIGHTS data project.

Get ready to work!

The V-Dem Varieties of Democracy "Personal Integrity Rights"
- FILE 1: V-Dem/V-Dem-CY-Core_csv_v13/codebook_v13.pdf
- FILE 2: V-Dem/V-Dem-CY-Core_csv_v13/V_Dem_Personal_Integrity_Rights-2022.xlsx

Syllabus Tasks
1. Review the V-Dem Varieties of Democracy Codebook *ONLY p30, 32-34, 40-42, 176-178*

2. Audit the "Personal Integrity Rights" scores for your country cases from last week

3. Spend some time exploring the dataset and find us something interesting to discuss





---

OLD SLIDES




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[
Have the average rates for the types of violations varied across time?
]

???



---

```{r, fig.retina=3, out.width='97%', fig.width=6, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.618}
## You make a line plot of averages across time for each measure
d |>
  pivot_longer(cols = polpris:kill, names_to = "Variables", values_to = "Values") |>
  group_by(year, Variables) |>
  summarize(
    Mean = mean(Values, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = Mean, color = Variables)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Mean Values",
       title = "Tracking Political Violence Averages Across Time") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0,2), xlim = c(1980, 2021)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = 0:2, labels = 0:2)  +
  guides(color = "none") +
  scale_color_brewer(type = "qual", palette = 2) +
  annotate("text", x = 2021, y = c(1.73, 1.2, 1.45, .65), label = c("Disap", "Kill", "Prison", "Torture"), color = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(4, name = "Dark2"))

```

???

Remember, lower values are bad!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-light_grey.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size55[
Focus on the scores for total index score in the years since 2001 and find us other examples of countries that have:

  + Improved the most, and
  
  + Regressed the most across the years in the sample
]

???



---

class: full, middle, slideblue

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', out.width='100%', fig.height=5, fig.width=9.5, eval=TRUE, cache=FALSE}
## Make a map

# Summarize data
d_diff <- d |>
  filter(year %in% c(2001, 2020)) |>
  select(country, iso_a3, year, physint_sum) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = physint_sum, names_prefix = "physint_") |>
  mutate(
    Difference = physint_2020 - physint_2001
  )

# Merge
d11 <- left_join(worldmap, d_diff, by = c("adm0_a3" = "iso_a3"))

# Audit missing data
# d_diff |>
#   select(country, Difference) |>
#   View()
# 
# tibble(d10) |>
#   select(name, Difference) |>
#   filter(is.na(Difference)) |>
#   print(n = 100)

# d10$Difference[d10$name == "Kosovo"] <- 7
# d10$Difference[d10$name == "S. Sudan"] <- 0
# d10$Difference[d10$name == "Serbia"] <- 6

d11 |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = Difference)) +
  labs(fill = "", title = "Changes in the Physical Integrity Index (2001 to 2020)") +
  #theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  #scale_fill_gradient(low = "red", high = "blue")
  scale_fill_gradient2()
```

???
